docs/dev/engineering-principles.md:29:- **DDx feature it shapes:** `ddx agent execute-bead` and `ddx work` (the queue drainer) — both create a new worktree per attempt and pass review findings forward only as explicit `<review-findings>` prompt sections, not as session state.
docs/dev/engineering-principles.md:35:- **Alternative rejected:** Stream execution telemetry to an external service (or stdout only) and treat the worktree as ephemeral. Rejected because it makes post-hoc review depend on external availability and prevents agents from reading their own prior attempts.
docs/dev/engineering-principles.md:36:- **Tradeoff:** Repository size grows with execution count, and bundles must be pruned periodically. In exchange, every bead has reviewable provenance and review agents can read prior-attempt evidence directly from the repo.
docs/dev/engineering-principles.md:41:- **Rule:** Start every attempt on the cheapest viable model or path. Escalate to a stronger model only when a review gate fails or the cheap path provably cannot meet the acceptance criteria.
docs/dev/engineering-principles.md:42:- **Decision generated:** `ddx work` drains the bead queue with a default harness configured for low-cost models. When a review finds blocking issues, the bead reopens and the next attempt is dispatched to a higher-capability harness with the prior review findings threaded in.
docs/dev/engineering-principles.md:45:- **DDx feature it shapes:** The `ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-loop` queue drainer with its review-gated escalation path, paired with per-harness model configuration in `.ddx/config.yaml`.

docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-001-spec-driven.md:32:The workflow begins with an idea—often vague and incomplete. Through iterative dialogue with AI, this idea becomes a comprehensive PRD. The AI asks clarifying questions, identifies edge cases, and helps define precise acceptance criteria. What might take days of meetings and documentation in traditional development happens in hours of focused specification work. This transforms the traditional SDLC—requirements and design become continuous activities rather than discrete phases. This is supportive of a **team process**, that's team reviewed-specifications are expressed and versioned, created in branches, and merged.
docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-001-spec-driven.md:214:These checklists force the LLM to self-review its output systematically, catching gaps that might otherwise slip through. It's like giving the LLM a quality assurance framework.

docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-013-mast-multi-agent-failures.md:17:Empirical study cataloguing how multi-agent LLM systems fail in practice — most failures are coordination/verification breakdowns, not raw model capability gaps. Justifies DDx's emphasis on explicit handoffs (beads), clear acceptance criteria, and review gates over loosely-coordinated agent swarms.
docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-015-frugalgpt.md:17:Seminal paper on cost-tiered LLM routing: cheap models attempt first, escalate to stronger models only when needed. Foundational reference for DDx's cost-tiered work pattern (cheap models do, strong models review) and Fizeau-style routing.
docs/helix/00-discover/references/REF-016-self-refine.md:18:Establishes iterative self-critique-and-revise as a robust quality-lifting pattern for LLMs. Underwrites HELIX's review gates and DDx's review-step escalation: generate, critique, revise — preferably with a stronger reviewer than producer.

cli/internal/agent/testdata/reviewer/bead-jsonl-oversized-line.jsonl:1:{"acceptance":"- [ ] JWT middleware installed in data-plane router as optional layer (runs when Authorization header present)\n- [ ] JwtIssuer constructed at startup in serve.rs (dev: random key with warning)\n- [ ] AuditAttribution populated on every audit write when ResolvedIdentity is present\n- [ ] gRPC service.rs migrated from x-axon-database to composite x-axon-tenant-database\n- [ ] gRPC service.rs gained optional JWT verify path\n- [ ] cutover_jwt_test.rs integration test with real JWT end-to-end\n- [ ] scripts/cutover-smoke.sh script runs end-to-end against dev server\n- [ ] cargo test --workspace green\n- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean\n- [ ] Legacy auth paths (--no-auth, --guest-role, Tailscale) still work unchanged","closing_commit_sha":"1a378fe785612ab9a24241977cf60c0c13e150b9","created_at":"2026-04-15T11:54:30.489788638Z","description":"Finish wiring the ADR-018 auth stack into the real request pipeline. After the smoke test on commit e83cf02, D3 already landed more than expected: `resolve_tenant_handler` composes `{tenant}:{database}` into the TenantRouter slug, giving real per-pair physical isolation. What remains is the three things that are *built but not installed*.\n\n## What is already live (confirmed by smoke test)\n\n- Path-based routing: /tenants/:t/databases/:d/... routed to per-pair SQLite files via composite slug\n- GraphQL under the nested path\n- Unprefixed REST + /graphql return 404\n- HttpAxonClient in the SDK + the UI use the new paths\n- Legacy X-Axon-Database header is gone from REST\n\n## What is still NOT installed (the gap this bead closes)\n\n### 1. JWT pipeline not enforced\n\nB1 landed `auth_pipeline::jwt_verify_layer` + `JwtIssuer`. The real gateway router in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` does NOT install this layer. The server currently runs in legacy auth mode (Tailscale or --no-auth). Install the layer as an OPTIONAL middleware: if the request has an Authorization header, run the JWT pipeline; if not, fall back to legacy auth.\n\nWire point: in `build_router_with_auth` (or wherever the data-plane nested router is constructed), add `.layer(from_fn_with_state(state.clone(), jwt_verify_layer))` before the tenant_handler middleware. The layer needs access to a `JwtIssuer` and the `StorageAdapter`. Add them to the AppState struct.\n\nCreate the JwtIssuer at server startup in `serve.rs`. Read the signing key from an env var `AXON_JWT_KEY` (base64 HMAC secret); if unset, generate a random 32-byte key at startup and log a warning (dev mode). Production key management is a future concern.\n\nThe legacy auth path (crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs Identity + Role) still works for requests without an Authorization header — the JWT layer is a no-op pass-through when the header is absent. This preserves --no-auth, --guest-role, and Tailscale whois behavior exactly as today.\n\n### 2. Audit attribution not populated\n\nF1 added `AuditAttribution` as an optional field on `AuditEntry` plus the `with_attribution()` builder. No handler currently calls it. In the JWT middleware (step 1), after `ResolvedIdentity` is installed into the request extension, also expose a `current_attribution()` helper on AppState that the audit write sites can call.\n\nFind every `AuditEntry::new(...)` call site in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` (there are probably 10-20) and chain `.with_attribution(current_attribution(\u0026request))` when a ResolvedIdentity is present. When absent (legacy auth), leave the attribution as None — the existing `actor: String` field still works.\n\n### 3. gRPC service.rs still reads x-axon-database\n\n`crates/axon-server/src/service.rs:26` has `const AXON_DATABASE_HEADER: \u0026str = \"x-axon-database\"` and `grpc_current_database` reads it from metadata. The gRPC service is entirely separate from the REST gateway and D3 did not touch it. Replace this header read with path-based extraction... except gRPC doesn't have URL paths in the same way. gRPC calls use service + method names, not paths. The database has to come from either:\n\n(a) A metadata field (still a header-equivalent), OR\n(b) A new field on every request proto\n\n(a) is pragmatic. Rename the metadata header from `x-axon-database` to `x-axon-tenant-database` and carry a composite value `\"{tenant}:{database}\"`. This matches the REST composite slug. The gRPC service parses it and dispatches via the same `get_or_create_any` path.\n\nAlso add `x-axon-authorization` (or reuse the standard `authorization` metadata key) for JWT credentials. Run the JWT verify pipeline in the gRPC `check_auth` step before dispatching to the handler.\n\nFor this bead, the gRPC change is small:\n- Rename the metadata constant\n- Update `grpc_current_database` to parse the composite form\n- Update the Rust + TS gRPC clients to send the composite form\n- Add JWT auth path in `check_auth`\n\n### 4. DatabaseRouter NOT swapped in (deferred — not needed right now)\n\nD2 landed `DatabaseRouter` as a new primitive, but the gateway still uses `TenantRouter::get_or_create_any(slug)`. The smoke test confirmed this composite slug works correctly. Replacing TenantRouter with DatabaseRouter is a refactor that doesn't change behavior — defer it to a later cleanup bead. Not in this bead's scope.\n\n## Scope summary\n\nThis bead does exactly three things:\n\n1. Install B1's JWT middleware in the data-plane router, optional path (Authorization header present)\n2. Populate F1's AuditAttribution at every audit write site when ResolvedIdentity is present\n3. Migrate gRPC service.rs from x-axon-database header to the composite slug + optional JWT metadata\n\n## Tests for this bead\n\n### Integration test: crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs (NEW)\n\n- create a JwtIssuer with a known key\n- mint a JWT for a default user with grants on (default, default)\n- start the in-process test server harness that gateway_test.rs uses\n- send a request with Authorization: Bearer \u003cjwt\u003e to /tenants/default/databases/default/collections\n- assert 200 (the JWT pipeline accepted + the handler saw ResolvedIdentity)\n\n### Integration test: attribution populated\n\n- send an entity-create request with a JWT\n- query the audit log\n- assert the audit entry has `attribution.user_id` set to the JWT sub\n\n### gRPC test: composite slug\n\n- send a CreateEntity gRPC request with metadata `x-axon-tenant-database: acme:orders`\n- assert routing to the acme:orders database (check the storage file exists)\n\n### Manual smoke test\n\nStart the server, issue a JWT via `/control/tenants/default/credentials`, use the HttpAxonClient with the JWT, create an entity, verify the audit log has attribution. Write a short shell script under `scripts/cutover-smoke.sh` that does this end-to-end.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- DatabaseRouter replacement — deferred\n- OIDC federation — follow-up\n- Signing key rotation — future ADR\n- Removing legacy Identity/Role types from auth.rs — they still serve the --no-auth and Tailscale paths\n\n## Files to touch\n\n- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — install JWT middleware, thread AuditAttribution into audit writes (10-20 sites)\n- crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs — construct JwtIssuer at startup\n- crates/axon-server/src/service.rs — rename header constant, parse composite, add JWT path\n- crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs — NEW\n- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh — NEW\n- Possibly sdk/typescript/src/client.ts — update gRPC metadata key if touching gRPC SDK surface\n\n## Files NOT to touch\n\n- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 is canonical, just consume it\n- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale path, out of scope for removal\n- crates/axon-storage/* — no storage changes\n- crates/axon-core/* — no type changes\n\n## Verification\n\n- cargo test -p axon-server (all prior tests green + new cutover_jwt_test)\n- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean\n- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh runs end-to-end against a dev cli/internal/skills/ddx/reference/review.md:3:DDx has two distinct review concepts. They are **not interchangeable**.
cli/internal/skills/ddx/reference/review.md:7:| **Bead review** | `ddx bead review <id>` | Grades a bead's implementation against its declared acceptance criteria. Per-AC verdict with evidence. |
cli/internal/skills/ddx/reference/review.md:8:| **Comparison/adversarial review** | workflow skill composition over `ddx run` | Dispatches review prompts across multiple roles or configured agent routes and aggregates the verdicts. Used for high-stakes or adversarial checks. |

cli/internal/agent/testdata/reviewer/reviewer-stream-oversized-body.jsonl:1:{"id":"as-bc4b0e90","timestamp":"2026-04-15T01:50:16.151938804Z","harness":"claude","surface":"claude","canonical_target":"claude-sonnet-4-6","model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","prompt_len":13868,"prompt":"\u003cexecute-bead\u003e\n  \u003cbead id=\"axon-dccbdf73\"\u003e\n    \u003ctitle\u003eauth-jwt-pipeline: sign + verify with revocation cache + request extension\u003c/title\u003e\n    \u003cdescription\u003e\nImplement the full JWT verification middleware in axon-server.\n\n## What you're building\n\nAn axum middleware layer that runs on every data-plane request. It validates the JWT, installs a (user_id, tenant_id, grants) tuple into the request extension, and returns a typed AuthError on any failure. Downstream handlers read the extension and trust it completely.\n\n## Scope — exactly what to add\n\n### New file: `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`\n\nA module exposing two things:\n\n1. A `JwtIssuer` helper that signs a `JwtClaims` into a compact JWT string. HS256 is the only algorithm required for this bead. The signing key is a `Vec\u003cu8\u003e` passed in at construction. Provide `issue(\u0026self, claims: \u0026JwtClaims) -\u003e Result\u003cString, AuthError\u003e` and a `verify(\u0026self, token: \u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003cJwtClaims, AuthError\u003e` for round-tripping in tests.\n\n2. An axum middleware `async fn jwt_verify_layer` that runs the 8-step verification order below. On success, installs a `ResolvedIdentity { user_id, tenant_id, grants }` into the request extension. On failure, returns a response built via `AuthError::into_response()` so the mapping to HTTP status + error.code is consistent.\n\nThe middleware also owns an `InMemoryRevocationCache` (a small `tokio::sync::RwLock\u003cHashSet\u003cUuid\u003e\u003e` is fine; nothing fancy) and falls back to SQL (via the storage adapter's new `is_jti_revoked` method) on cache miss.\n\n### Extend: `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs`\n\n- Add `ResolvedIdentity { pub user_id: UserId, pub tenant_id: TenantId, pub grants: Grants }`.\n- Add `impl AuthError` with `pub fn status_code(\u0026self) -\u003e u16` returning 401 or 403 per ADR-018 §4 failure table, and `pub fn error_code(\u0026self) -\u003e \u0026'static str` returning the stable code string. (An axum `IntoResponse` impl can live in axon-server.)\n- Add `impl Grants` with `pub fn find_database(\u0026self, name: \u0026str) -\u003e Option\u003c\u0026GrantedDatabase\u003e` and `impl GrantedDatabase` with `pub fn has_op(\u0026self, op: Op) -\u003e bool`.\n\n### Extend: `crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs` (trait)\n\nAdd one method to the `StorageAdapter` trait:\n\n```rust\nasync fn is_jti_revoked(\u0026self, jti: Uuid) -\u003e Result\u003cbool, AxonError\u003e;\n```\n\nImplement it in `crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs` (store a `HashSet\u003cUuid\u003e`; default-empty), `crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs`, and `crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs` (`SELECT 1 FROM credential_revocations WHERE jti = $1`). The A1 schema (`auth_schema.rs`) already has the `credential_revocations` table — just query it.\n\n### Wire into: `crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs`\n\nRe-export `auth_pipeline::{jwt_verify_layer, JwtIssuer, InMemoryRevocationCache}`. Don't wire it into the main router yet — that's D-phase work. For this bead, the middleware just needs to exist and be testable in isolation.\n\n## Verification order (normative — ADR-018 §4)\n\n1. Extract `Authorization: Bearer \u003cjwt\u003e` from the request. Missing header → `AuthError::Unauthenticated` (401). The `--no-auth` bypass is NOT part of this bead — that's E2.\n2. Parse and verify the JWT signature with HS256 against the issuer's key. Signature invalid → `CredentialInvalid` (401). Malformed structure → `CredentialMalformed` (401). `aud` as a JSON array → `CredentialMalformed` (this falls out of the A2 `parse_claims` wrapper — use it).\n3. Check `nbf` and `exp` against `SystemTime::now()` with a 30s skew budget. `exp` more than 30s in the past → `CredentialExpired` (401). `nbf` more than 30s in the future → `CredentialNotYetValid` (401).\n4. Check `claims.jti` against the revocation cache, then SQL on miss. Hit → `CredentialRevoked` (401). Populate the cache on SQL hit to speed up subsequent checks.\n5. Compare `claims.aud` to the `{tenant}` segment extracted from the URL path. Mismatch → `CredentialWrongTenant` (**403**, not 401 — see the table). The URL extraction path is: look at `request.uri().path()` and parse the leading `/tenants/{tenant}/databases/{database}/...` segments. For this bead, implement a small helper `fn extract_tenant_database(path: \u0026str) -\u003e Option\u003c(\u0026str, \u0026str)\u003e` that returns `None` for non-data-plane paths so they skip the middleware.\n6. Look up the `sub` UUID in the `users` table via a new `StorageAdapter::get_user(UserId) -\u003e Result\u003cOption\u003cUser\u003e, AxonError\u003e` method. User not found or `user.suspended_at_ms.is_some()` → `UserSuspended` (401).\n7. Check tenant membership: a new `StorageAdapter::get_tenant_member(TenantId, UserId) -\u003e Result\u003cOption\u003cTenantMember\u003e, AxonError\u003e`. Missing → `NotATenantMember` (403).\n8. Walk `claims.grants.databases[]` looking for an entry matching the URL `{database}`. None → `DatabaseNotGranted` (403). Found → compute the required op from the HTTP method (GET = Read, POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE = Write) and intersect with the grant's ops. Empty intersection → `OpNotGranted` (403). Admin-requiring routes are out of scope for this bead — the middleware only handles Read/Write classification.\n9. Build `ResolvedIdentity` and install it via `request.extensions_mut().insert(resolved)`.\n\n## Error mapping — must exactly match ADR-018 §4\n\nThe mapping table is copied here verbatim. Do not invent variants; the 14 AuthError variants already exist in axon-core.\n\n| Failure | Status | `error.code` |\n|---|---|---|\n| No Authorization header | 401 | `unauthenticated` |\n| Header not `Bearer \u003ctoken\u003e` or base64 broken | 401 | `credential_malformed` |\n| JWT structurally invalid / missing claims | 401 | `credential_malformed` |\n| `aud` as JSON array | 401 | `credential_malformed` |\n| Signature invalid | 401 | `credential_invalid` |\n| `exp` in past (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_expired` |\n| `nbf` in future (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_not_yet_valid` |\n| `jti` revoked | 401 | `credential_revoked` |\n| `iss` unknown | 401 | `credential_foreign_issuer` — for this bead, reject if `claims.iss != issuer.issuer_id` |\n| `aud` ≠ URL tenant | **403** | `credential_wrong_tenant` |\n| `sub` suspended/deleted | 401 | `user_suspended` |\n| `sub` not a tenant member | **403** | `not_a_tenant_member` |\n| URL database not in grants | **403** | `database_not_granted` |\n| Op not in grant | **403** | `op_not_granted` |\n\nNote the 401 vs 403 split: credential-level problems are 401, scope-level problems are 403.\n\n## Testing — all must pass for this bead to be complete\n\n### Unit tests in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_test.rs`\n\nFor every row of the error mapping table, build the minimal defective request and assert the middleware produces exactly that `(status, error_code)` pair. Table-driven with `#[test]` or a single `#[test]` that iterates an array of cases.\n\nAdditionally:\n\n- `jwt_roundtrip_succeeds`: issue a valid JWT with `JwtIssuer::issue`, verify it with `JwtIssuer::verify`, assert claims match.\n- `valid_jwt_populates_request_extension`: run the full middleware on a valid JWT, assert the response is 200 and the handler sees `ResolvedIdentity` in extensions.\n- `revocation_takes_effect_within_one_second`: issue a valid JWT, verify it succeeds, insert the jti into the revocation cache, verify the next request returns 401 `credential_revoked`. Do NOT use sleep — invalidate the cache directly.\n- `clock_skew_30s_accepted_31s_rejected`: construct a JWT with `exp` at `now - 29s` (should succeed) and one with `exp` at `now - 31s` (should fail with `credential_expired`). Do the same for `nbf`.\n- `ops_matrix`: for each HTTP method in {GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE} and each op set in the grant, assert the expected 200/403 outcome.\n\n### Integration test in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_integration_test.rs`\n\nBuild a minimal axum router with one dummy route `GET /tenants/acme/databases/orders/ping`. Install the middleware. Hit it with:\n- No auth header → 401 `unauthenticated`\n- Valid JWT with `grants: [{name: \"orders\", ops: [read]}]` → 200\n- Valid JWT with the wrong tenant in aud → 403 `credential_wrong_tenant`\n- Valid JWT with grants for a different database → 403 `database_not_granted`\n\n### axon-sim workload `inv_018_grant_enforcement`\n\n**If this is too large for one pass, skip the axon-sim workload and note it in the commit message for a follow-up bead.** The core verification must ship; the simulation workload can follow. Do not block the whole bead on the workload.\n\n## Crate dependencies you will need\n\nAdd to `crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml` under `[dependencies]`:\n```toml\njsonwebtoken = \"9\"\n```\n\nEverything else (axum, tower, tokio, uuid, serde_json, http) is already in the workspace.\n\n## Context you must know about\n\n### A1 landed: schema\n- `crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs` has the SQL for `users`, `user_identities`, `tenant_users`, `credential_revocations`, etc.\n- `user_identities.external_id` (NOT `subject`) — already corrected.\n- Migration runs via `apply_auth_migrations_sqlite` / `_postgres`.\n\n### A2 landed: types\n- `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` has all the types: `TenantId`, `UserId`, `User`, `UserIdentity`, `Tenant`, `TenantMember`, `TenantRole`, `Op`, `GrantedDatabase`, `Grants`, `JwtClaims`, `AuthError` (14 variants).\n- `parse_claims(\u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003cJwtClaims, AuthError\u003e` wraps serde_json and maps errors to `CredentialMalformed`. USE IT.\n- `AUTH_ERROR_VARIANT_COUNT == 14` is enforced via compile-time exhaustive match.\n- Delegation helper on `TenantRole`: `admin.can_delegate(op)` / etc.\n\n### Files you should NOT touch\n- Anything outside `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`, `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` (additive), `crates/axon-storage/src/{adapter,memory,sqlite,postgres}.rs` (additive), and the two new test files.\n- The existing `crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs` — that's the Tailscale whois path. Leave it alone. Your pipeline is a new module.\n\n## Quality bar\n\nYou are the implementation step. A post-merge review with opus will verify the result against the AC. If your commit misses an AC item, the bead reopens and escalates to a higher tier. **Don't commit partial work hoping the review slides.** If you cannot complete the bead in this pass, write a `no_changes_rationale.txt` explaining exactly what's done and what's blocking, and do NOT commit — the bead will be re-queued.\n\nSpecifically: if you cannot make `cargo test -p axon-server` and `cargo clippy -p axon-server -- -D warnings` green, do not commit. Axon server — HTTP gateway, gRPC service, MCP stdio, and authentication.\\n2\\t//!\\n3\\t//! # Authentication\\n4\\t//!\\n5\\t//! Identity is resolved once per request by [`auth::AuthContext`] and\\n6\\t//! injected as a typed extension.  Three modes are supported:\\n7\\t//!\\n8\\t//! | Mode | Flag | Actor | Role |\\n9\\t//! |------|------|-------|------|\\n10\\t//! | `NoAuth` | `--no-auth` | `\\\"anonymous\\\"` | Admin |\\n11\\t//! | `Tailscale` | *(default)* | node name | from ACL tags |\\n12\\t//! | `Guest` | `--guest-role \u003crole\u003e` | `\\\"guest\\\"` | configured role |\\n13\\t//!\\n14\\t//! In `Tailscale` mode the server contacts the local Tailscale daemon over its\\n15\\t//! Unix socket (`/run/tailscale/tailscaled.sock` by default) and calls\\n16\\t//! `/localapi/v0/whois?addr=\u003cpeer\u003e` to resolve the connecting node's identity.\\n17\\t//! Resolved identities are cached by peer IP for the duration of\\n18\\t//! `--auth-cache-ttl-secs` (default 60 s).\\n19\\t//!\\n20\\t//! ACL tag → role mapping:\\n21\\t//! - `tag:axon-admin` → [`Role::Admin`]\\n22\\t//! - `tag:axon-write` / `tag:axon-agent` → [`Role::Write`]\\n23\\t//! - `tag:axon-read` → [`Role::Read`]\\n24\\t//! - no matching tag → `--tailscale-default-role` (default `read`)\\n25\\t//!\\n26\\t//! Connections that are not on the tailnet are rejected with HTTP 401 / gRPC\\n27\\t//! `UNAUTHENTICATED`.  If the Tailscale daemon is unreachable the server\\n28\\t//! returns HTTP 503 / gRPC `UNAVAILABLE`.\\n29\\t//!\\n30\\t//! # Request flow\\n31\\t//!\\n32\\t//! ```text\\n33\\t//! TCP accept\\n34\\t//!   └─ authenticate_http_request  (gateway.rs middleware)\\n35\\t//!        ├─ AuthContext::resolve_peer  →  Identity\\n36\\t//!        └─ insert Identity into request extensions\\n37\\t//!             └─ route handler\\n38\\t//!                  ├─ extract Extension\u003cIdentity\u003e\\n39\\t//!                  ├─ identity.require_read() / require_write() / require_admin()\\n40\\t//!                  └─ handler logic\\n41\\t//! ```\\n42\\t//!\\n43\\t//! gRPC follows the same pattern via [`service::AxonServiceImpl::authorize`].\\n44\\tpub mod self.collection_exists_in_namespace(collection, namespace)\\n    }\\n}\\n\\n// ── Per-tenant PostgreSQL provisioning ───────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n/// Validate a PostgreSQL database name to prevent SQL injection in DDL.\\n///\\n/// PostgreSQL identifiers may contain letters, digits, `_`, and `$`, and\\n/// must start with a letter or `_`. We apply a conservative subset of that\\n/// rule here to keep things simple and safe.\\nfn validate_pg_db_name(name: \u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003c(), AxonError\u003e {\\n    if name.is_empty() {\\n        return Err(AxonError::InvalidArgument(\\n            \\\"database name must not be empty\\\".to_owned(),\\n        ));\\n    }\\n    let first = name.chars().next().expect(\\\"non-empty checked above\\\");\\n    if !first.is_ascii_alphabetic() \u0026\u0026 first != '_' {\\n        return Err(AxonError::InvalidArgument(format!(\\n            \\\"database name '{name}' must start with a letter or underscore\\\"\\n        )));\\n    }\\n    if !name.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '_') {\\n        return Err(AxonError::InvalidArgument(format!(\\n            \\\"database name '{name}' contains invalid characters (only ASCII alphanumeric and _ allowed)\\\"\\n        )));\\n    }\\n    Ok(())\\n}\\n\\n/// Derive a per-tenant DSN from a superadmin DSN by replacing/adding the\\n/// `dbname` key.\\n///\\n/// The superadmin DSN uses the `postgres` maintenance database.  We produce a\\n/// new DSN that targets `axon_{name}`.\\n///\\n/// Both libpq keyword–value format (`host=... dbname=...`) and URL format\\n/// (`postgres://...`) are supported.\\npub fn tenant_dsn(superadmin_dsn: \u0026str, tenant_db_name: \u0026str) -\u003e String {\\n    let target = format!(\\\"axon_{tenant_db_name}\\\");\\n    if superadmin_dsn.starts_with(\\\"postgres://\\\") || superadmin_dsn.starts_with(\\\"postgresql://\\\") {\\n        // URL format: replace path component.\\n        // e.g. postgres://user:pass@host/somedb?sslmode=disable\\n        if let Some(pos) = superadmin_dsn.find(\\\"://\\\") {\\n            let after_scheme = \u0026superadmin_dsn[pos + 3..];\\n            // Find the slash that separates authority from path.\\n            if let Some(slash_pos) = after_scheme.find('/') {\\n                let scheme_authority = \u0026superadmin_dsn[..pos + 3 + slash_pos];\\n                let rest = \u0026after_scheme[slash_pos + 1..];\\n                // Strip existing dbname (everything before '?').\\n                let query = if let Some(q) = rest.find('?') {\\n                    \u0026rest[q..]\\n                } else {\\n                    \\\"\\\"\\n                };\\n                return format!(\\\"{scheme_authority}/{target}{query}\\\");\\n            }\\n            // No path: append the target database.\\n            return format!(\\\"{superadmin_dsn}/{target}\\\");\\n        }\\n        format!(\\\"{superadmin_dsn}/{target}\\\")\\n    } else {\\n        // Keyword–value format.  Replace existing dbname= or append it.\\n        let mut parts: Vec\u003cString\u003e = superadmin_dsn\\n            .split_whitespace()\\n            .filter(|part| !part.starts_with(\\\"dbname=\\\"))\\n            .map(str::to_owned)\\n            .collect();\\n        parts.push(format!(\\\"dbname={target}\\\"));\\n        parts.join(\\\" \\\")\\n    }\\n}\\n\\n/// Create a physical PostgreSQL database named `axon_{name}` using a\\n/// superadmin connection.\\n///\\n/// The `superadmin_dsn` must connect to the `postgres` maintenance database\\n/// (or any database the superuser can reach) with sufficient privileges to\\n/// execute `CREATE DATABASE`.\\n///\\n/// The database name is validated to prevent SQL injection.\\n///\\n/// # Errors\\n///\\n/// Returns `AxonError::AlreadyExists` if the database already exists.\\n/// Returns `AxonError::InvalidArgument` if `name` contains invalid characters.\\n/// Returns `AxonError::Storage` for other PostgreSQL errors.\\npub fn provision_postgres_database(superadmin_dsn: \u0026str, name: \u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003c(), AxonError\u003e {\\n    validate_pg_db_name(name)?;\\n    let full_name = format!(\\\"axon_{name}\\\");\\n    let rt = tokio::runtime::Builder::new_current_thread()\\n        .enable_all()\\n        .build()\\n        .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\\n    rt.block_on(async {\\n        let (client, connection) = tokio_postgres::connect(superadmin_dsn, NoTls)\\n            .await\\n            .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\\n        tokio::spawn(async move {\\n            let _ = connection.await;\\n        });\\n        let row = client\\n            .query_one(\\n                \\\"SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM pg_database WHERE datname = $1)\\\",\\n                \u0026[\u0026full_name.as_str()],\\n            )\\n            .await\\n            .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\\n        let exists: bool = row.get(0);\\n        if exists {\\n            return Err(AxonError::AlreadyExists(format!(\\n                \\\"PostgreSQL database '{full_name}'\\\"\\n            )));\\n        }\\n        // CREATE DATABASE cannot run inside a transaction; use simple_query to\\n        // avoid the implicit transaction that execute() would open.\\n        client\\n            .simple_query(\u0026format!(\\\"CREATE DATABASE \\\\\\\"{full_name}\\\\\\\"\\\"))\\n            .await\\n            .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\\n        Ok(())\\n    })\\n}\\n\\n/// Drop the physical PostgreSQL database named `axon_{name}`.\\n///\\n/// The `superadmin_dsn` must have sufficient privileges characters.\\n/// Returns `AxonError::Storage` for other PostgreSQL errors.\\npub fn deprovision_postgres_database(superadmin_dsn: \u0026str, name: \u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003c(), AxonError\u003e {\\n    validate_pg_db_name(name)?;\\n    let full_name = format!(\\\"axon_{name}\\\");\\n    let rt = tokio::runtime::Builder::new_current_thread()\\n        .enable_all()\\n        .build()\\n        .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\\n    rt.block_on(async {\\n        let (client, connection) = tokio_postgres::connect(superadmin_dsn, NoTls)\\n            .await\\n using HS256.\\npub struct JwtIssuer {\\n    secret: Vec\u003cu8\u003e,\\n    /// Issuer identifier placed in the `iss` claim and verified on every request.\\n    pub issuer_id: String,\\n}\\n\\nimpl JwtIssuer {\\n    /// Create a new issuer with the given HMAC secret and issuer ID.\\n    pub fn new(secret: Vec\u003cu8\u003e, issuer_id: String) -\u003e Self {\\n        Self { secret, issuer_id }\\n    }\\n\\n    /// Sign `claims` into a compact HS256 JWT string.\\n    pub fn issue(\u0026self, claims: \u0026JwtClaims) -\u003e Result\u003cString, AuthError\u003e {\\n        encode(\\n with a configurable skew budget.\\n    pub fn verify(\u0026self, token: \u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003cJwtClaims, AuthError\u003e {\\n        let mut validation = Validation::new(Algorithm::HS256);\\n   jsonwebtoken::errors::ErrorKind::InvalidSignature =\u003e AuthError::CredentialInvalid,\\n                jsonwebtoken::errors::ErrorKind::Json(_) =\u003e AuthError::CredentialMalformed,\\n                jsonwebtoken::errors::ErrorKind::Base64(_) =\u003e AuthError::CredentialMalformed,\\n                jsonwebtoken::errors::ErrorKind::Utf8(_) =\u003e AuthError::CredentialMalformed,\\n                jsonwebtoken::errors::ErrorKind::InvalidToken =\u003e AuthError::CredentialMalformed,\\n                _ =\u003e AuthError::CredentialMalformed,\\n            })\\n    }\\n}\\n\\n// ── InMemoryRevocationCache ───────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n/// In-process cache of revoked JWT IDs.\\n///\\n/// The middleware checks this first before falling back to the storage adapter,\\n/// avoiding a round-trip to storage on every request for known-revoked tokens.\\n#[derive(Default)]\\npub struct InMemoryRevocationCache {\\n    inner: RwLock\u003cHashSet\u003cUuid\u003e\u003e,\\n}\\n\\nimpl InMemoryRevocationCache {\\n    /// Create an empty cache.\\n    pub fn new() -\u003e Self {\\n        Self::default()\\n    }\\n\\n    /// request.extensions_mut().insert(identity);\\n            next.run(request).await\\n        }\\n        Err(err) =\u003e AuthErrorResponse(err).into_response(),\\n    }\\n}\\n\\n// ── Core verification logic ───────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\nfn verify_request(\\n    state: \u0026AuthPipelineState,\\n    headers: \u0026HeaderMap,\\n    method: \u0026Method,\\n    tenant: \u0026str,\\n    database: \u0026str,\\n) -\u003e Result\u003cResolvedIdentity, AuthError\u003e {\\n    // Step 1: Extract Bearer token.\\n    let token = extract_bearer_token(headers)?;\\n\\n    // .unwrap_or_default()\\n        .as_secs();\\n    const SKEW: u64 = 30;\\n    // Expired: exp + skew \u003c now  ↔  expired more than `skew` seconds ago\\n    if claims.exp.saturating_add(SKEW) \u003c let user = {\\n        let storage = state\\n            .storage\\n            .lock()\\n            .map_err(|_| AuthError::Unauthenticated)?;\\n        storage\\n Err(AuthError::UserSuspended);\\n    }\\n\\n    // Step 8: Check tenant membership.\\n    let tenant_id = TenantId::new(tenant);\\n    {\\n        let storage = state\\n            .storage\\n            .lock()\\n            .map_err(|_| AuthError::Unauthenticated)?;\\n        storage\\n            .get_tenant_member(tenant_id.clone(), user_id.clone())\\n            .map_err(|_| AuthError::NotATenantMember)?\\n            .ok_or(AuthError::NotATenantMember)?;\\n    }\\n\\n    // Step 9: Walk grants for the URL database + HTTP-method-derived op.\\n    let granted_db = claims\\n        .grants\\n        .find_database(database)\\n        .ok_or(AuthError::DatabaseNotGranted)?;\\n\\n    let required_op = if method == Method::GET {\\n        Op::Read\\n    } else {\\n        Op::Write\\n    };\\n    if !granted_db.has_op(required_op) {\\n        return Err(AuthError::OpNotGranted);\\n    }\\n\\n    Ok(ResolvedIdentity {\\n        user_id,\\n        tenant_id,\\n (parts.next(), parts.next(), parts.next(), parts.next()) {\\n        (Some(\\\"tenants\\\"), Some(tenant), Some(\\\"databases\\\"), Some(rest))\\n            if !tenant.is_empty() \u0026\u0026 directory for this skill: /home/erik/.claude/skills/helix-commit\\n\\n# Commit: HELIX-Compliant Git Commit\\n\\nCreate a well-formatted commit with issue traceability, run the project's\\nbuild gate, push, and optionally update the tracker.\\n\\n## When to Use\\n\\n- After implementing a tracked issue\\n- When you need a properly formatted commit message\\n- When codex or another agent needs commit support\\n\\n## What It Does\\n\\n1. **Inspect** staged changes (`git diff --cached --stat`)\\n2. **Format** commit message:\\n   - First line: `\u003cissue-id\u003e \u003cconcise summary\u003e` (under 72 chars)\\n   - Body: what changed and why, governing artifact refs\\n   - Trailer: verification commands run\\n3. **Pre-push gate**: run the project's build check before pushing\\n   - `lefthook run pre-commit` if available\\n   - `cargo check --workspace` for Rust projects\\n   - `npm test` for Node.js projects\\n4. **Commit and push**: `git commit`, `git pull --rebase`, `git push`\\n5. **Tracker update** (if issue-id provided): close the issue\\n\\n## Commit Message Format\\n\\n```\\nhx-abc123 Short summary of the change\\n\\nLonger description of what changed and why. docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-02-fictional-upstream-deps.md:15:**Trigger incident**: Phase 2 Story 10 bead batch filed with hard `dep` edges and bead descriptions claiming "blocked on Fizeau CONTRACT-003 extension for `ExcludePinned` + `ProviderID` + `ProviderClass` + `ActualPower` + separate review cost." On user push-back, verification showed those fields were either already shipped in `fizeau v0.9.28` or never necessary.
docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-02-fictional-upstream-deps.md:19:A planning sub-agent (codex review of the Story 10 plan, recorded at `/tmp/story-10-codex.out`) recommended adding several fields to the upstream Fizeau `ExecuteRequest` and `ExecuteResponse` to support reviewer pairing and capability-tier escalation. The recommendation was framed as a "hard upstream dependency" gating six DDx beads (S10_3, S10_5, S10_6, S10_9, S12_1, and indirectly others through coupling).
docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-02-fictional-upstream-deps.md:33:| `ExcludePinned` field on `ExecuteRequest` | Never needed — design rejected; reviewer "different provider" is best-effort emergent from `MinPower` bump |
docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-02-fictional-upstream-deps.md:35:| Separate review cost field | Never needed — `CostUSD` already accumulates per-call |
docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-02-fictional-upstream-deps.md:48:When a planning sub-agent generates a review against an under-specified plan, it can confidently recommend upstream API additions that are either already shipped or never necessary. The sub-agent's review reads as authoritative; downstream bead descriptions inherit the framing without independent verification. The pattern propagates because:
docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-02-fictional-upstream-deps.md:50:1. The DDx-side bead author (planning agent or human) trusts the codex review without cross-checking the upstream module's `go.mod` version, contract spec, and historical bead state
docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-02-fictional-upstream-deps.md:60:3. **Push back on the planning sub-agent.** Codex (and any plan-review agent) does not always check shipped state. When its review is built on a hypothetical upstream world, the right move is to challenge the premise, not file beads against the hypothesis.
docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-05-02-fictional-upstream-deps.md:90:- Should the multi-model plan-review pattern be amended so codex/opus reviews of plans that touch upstream contracts include an explicit "verify shipped state of named fields" step before producing the review? docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:246:| `review_fixable_gap` with retry budget remaining | no durable status change if continuing immediately; otherwise `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add no human label unless retry budget exhausted | `review-fixable-gap` + retry decision | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref; next cycle records `repair_context_from_review_group` |

docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:251:| `review_error` exhausted | `in_progress → blocked` | add `needs_human` | `review-manual-required` | `Extra["last-review-error"]` carries class, reviewer slot, and attempt count |

ddx:
  id: TD-036
  depends_on:
    - FEAT-027
  status: draft
---
# Technical Design: Prose Quality Pipeline

## Status

Draft. This TD closes the implementation-boundary gap left open by
FEAT-027 by deciding how DDx evaluates prose deterministically, how the
default plugin packages the assets, and how the first command surface
behaves when optional tooling is unavailable.

## Why This TD Exists

FEAT-027 defines the product problem and the required output shape for
deterministic prose checks, but it intentionally stops short of naming
the execution boundary. That leaves three open questions for the first
implementation bead:

- whether DDx should shell out to Vale
- whether DDx should embed a small checker
- whether DDx should wrap a pluggable runner and treat external tooling
  as optional

This TD answers the checker boundary question so the implementation
beads can start from a stable contract instead of inventing their own
architecture.

## Decision

DDx should ship a pluggable runner wrapper whose canonical first runner
is an embedded checker.

- The deterministic prose logic lives in DDx, not in Vale.
- Vale is an optional compatibility runner, not the required core.
- The wrapper is what lets DDx keep the first surface advisory, select a
  runner based on configuration, and degrade gracefully when external
  tooling is missing.

That boundary keeps the product contract stable:

- the checker is deterministic and explainable
- the first executable surface does not depend on a third-party install
- optional tooling can be added later without changing the finding
  schema

## Runtime Behavior

### Default path

The default path is the embedded checker. It runs in-process and does not
require Vale or any other external binary.

### Install behavior

The default DDx plugin should ship the embedded checker assets and their
rules/vocabulary/fixture tree. It should not require the user to install Vale
for the first supported surface to run.

### Optional runner path

The wrapper may delegate to Vale when a project explicitly selects that
runner. That path is compatibility-oriented and must produce the same
finding schema as the embedded checker.

### Missing-tool behavior

If the selected optional runner is unavailable, DDx must not turn the
prose check into a hard failure by default.

- In `policy: advisory`, DDx falls back to the embedded checker when it
  can, or reports a single advisory diagnostic that the optional runner
  was unavailable.
- In `policy: blocking`, DDx still prefers to run the embedded checker
  so the user gets concrete findings; an unavailable optional runner is
  reported as an execution diagnostic, not as a prose finding.
- When fallback is possible, the command should still return the
  embedded checker findings and keep the runner-missing diagnostic
  separate from the finding stream.

The important rule is that missing optional tooling never erases the
document analysis path. It only changes whether DDx can use the selected
runner implementation. The first executable surface stays advisory by
default even when the runner is optional: the user still gets findings,
and missing-tool state is surfaced separately as an execution diagnostic
instead of suppressing the scan.

## Default Plugin Asset Layout

The prose-quality assets belong in the default DDx plugin, not in a
project-specific check scaffold.

Proposed source layout:

- `library/checks/prose-quality/check.yaml`
- `library/checks/prose-quality/rules/`
- `library/checks/prose-quality/vocabulary/`
- `library/checks/prose-quality/fixtures/`

Installed layout:

- `.ddx/plugins/ddx/checks/prose-quality/check.yaml`
- `.ddx/plugins/ddx/checks/prose-quality/rules/`
- `.ddx/plugins/ddx/checks/prose-quality/vocabulary/`
- `.ddx/plugins/ddx/checks/prose-quality/fixtures/`

Layout rules:

- `rules/` stores named rule definitions grouped by mode.
- `vocabulary/` stores project terminology that the checker should
  preserve or prefer.
- `fixtures/` stores golden inputs and expected findings for regression
  tests.
- `check.yaml` wires the default command invocation and the runner
  selection defaults.

The layout is intentionally check-shaped rather than skill-shaped. The
skill can point authors at the workflow, but the asset tree owns the
deterministic rule definitions.

## Config Schema Sketch

The config schema needs to expose the policy knobs without making the
first release overfit to one runner.

```yaml
prose:
  mode: technical | planning | public
  severity: advisory | warning | blocking
  policy: advisory | blocking
  runner: embedded | vale | auto
  includes:
    - docs/helix/**
  excludes:
    - "**/*.generated.md"
  vocabulary:
    accept:
      - DDx
      - bead
      - execution
    reject:
      - system
      - solution
      - seamless
```

Semantics:

- `mode` selects the rule pack.
- `severity` is the severity attached to emitted findings.
- `policy` controls whether findings are advisory by default or can be
  elevated to blocking later.
- `runner` selects the implementation boundary.
- `includes` and `excludes` define the text selection scope.
- `vocabulary.accept` preserves project terms and domain terms.
- `vocabulary.reject` names generic substitutes the checker should flag
  when they replace project vocabulary.

`policy: advisory` is the default. That is the product-level default
required by FEAT-027 and the default the first executable surface must
honor.

## CLI Shape

The CLI surface is intentionally small.

### Primary command

`ddx doc prose --changed`

This is the first supported surface. It checks changed prose only and is
the default entry point for pre-review and pre-merge usage.

### Future direct-path command

`ddx doc prose <paths>`

This is the future explicit-path form. It should accept one or more
paths, reuse the same engine, and allow a caller to target a fixed set of
documents without relying on the diff.

### Shared behavior

Both forms must:

- load the same rule pack and vocabulary assets
- emit the same finding schema
- respect the same advisory/blocking policy
- preserve the same mode-specific rule selection

The only difference is how the input set is selected.

## Finding Schema

Findings must be structural and machine-readable. The canonical fields
are:

- `file`
- `line` or `line_range`
- `rule_id`
- `severity`
- `rationale`
- `suggested_edit`

Each finding therefore carries file, line, and rule identifiers together
with an explanation and a concrete edit suggestion.

Implementation may add helper fields such as `mode`, `snippet`, or
`runner`, but these core fields must remain stable.

Rules for each field:

- `file` is the relative path of the affected document.
- `line` or `line_range` identifies the touched text span.
- `rule_id` is a stable deterministic identifier, not a prose label.
- `severity` reflects the configured policy and the rule’s native
  impact.
- `rationale` must explain why the rule fired using observed text.
- `suggested_edit` must propose a concrete rewrite, replacement, or
  deletion.

The FEAT-027 principle applies here too: the output must be specific
enough that a later review consumer can reuse it without changing the
rule model.

## Fixture And Golden Test Plan

The first implementation bead should be guided by fixture-driven golden
tests rather than ad hoc assertions.

Recommended fixture set:

- one technical sample with vague claims and uncoupled abstractions
- one planning sample with generic roadmap language
- one public sample with voice drift and filler phrases
- one sample with accepted project vocabulary that must be preserved

cli/internal/agent/testdata/reviewer/bead-jsonl-oversized-line.jsonl:1:{"acceptance":"- [ ] JWT middleware installed in data-plane router as optional layer (runs when Authorization header present)
- [ ] JwtIssuer constructed at startup in serve.rs (dev: random key with warning)
- [ ] AuditAttribution populated on every audit write when ResolvedIdentity is present
- [ ] gRPC service.rs migrated from x-axon-database to composite x-axon-tenant-database
- [ ] gRPC service.rs gained optional JWT verify path
- [ ] cutover_jwt_test.rs integration test with real JWT end-to-end
- [ ] scripts/cutover-smoke.sh script runs end-to-end against dev server
- [ ] cargo test --workspace green
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean
- [ ] Legacy auth paths (--no-auth, --guest-role, Tailscale) still work unchanged","closing_commit_sha":"1a378fe785612ab9a24241977cf60c0c13e150b9","created_at":"2026-04-15T11:54:30.489788638Z","description":"Finish wiring the ADR-018 auth stack into the real request pipeline. After the smoke test on commit e83cf02, D3 already landed more than expected: `resolve_tenant_handler` composes `{tenant}:{database}` into the TenantRouter slug, giving real per-pair physical isolation. What remains is the three things that are *built but not installed*.

## What is already live (confirmed by smoke test)

- Path-based routing: /tenants/:t/databases/:d/... routed to per-pair SQLite files via composite slug
- GraphQL under the nested path
- Unprefixed REST + /graphql return 404
- HttpAxonClient in the SDK + the UI use the new paths
- Legacy X-Axon-Database header is gone from REST

## What is still NOT installed (the gap this bead closes)

### 1. JWT pipeline not enforced

B1 landed `auth_pipeline::jwt_verify_layer` + `JwtIssuer`. The real gateway router in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` does NOT install this layer. The server currently runs in legacy auth mode (Tailscale or --no-auth). Install the layer as an OPTIONAL middleware: if the request has an Authorization header, run the JWT pipeline; if not, fall back to legacy auth.

Wire point: in `build_router_with_auth` (or wherever the data-plane nested router is constructed), add `.layer(from_fn_with_state(state.clone(), jwt_verify_layer))` before the tenant_handler middleware. The layer needs access to a `JwtIssuer` and the `StorageAdapter`. Add them to the AppState struct.

Create the JwtIssuer at server startup in `serve.rs`. Read the signing key from an env var `AXON_JWT_KEY` (base64 HMAC secret); if unset, generate a random 32-byte key at startup and log a warning (dev mode). Production key management is a future concern.

The legacy auth path (crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs Identity + Role) still works for requests without an Authorization header — the JWT layer is a no-op pass-through when the header is absent. This preserves --no-auth, --guest-role, and Tailscale whois behavior exactly as today.

### 2. Audit attribution not populated

F1 added `AuditAttribution` as an optional field on `AuditEntry` plus the `with_attribution()` builder. No handler currently calls it. In the JWT middleware (step 1), after `ResolvedIdentity` is installed into the request extension, also expose a `current_attribution()` helper on AppState that the audit write sites can call.

Find every `AuditEntry::new(...)` call site in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` (there are probably 10-20) and chain `.with_attribution(current_attribution(&request))` when a ResolvedIdentity is present. When absent (legacy auth), leave the attribution as None — the existing `actor: String` field still works.

### 3. gRPC service.rs still reads x-axon-database

`crates/axon-server/src/service.rs:26` has `const AXON_DATABASE_HEADER: &str = "x-axon-database"` and `grpc_current_database` reads it from metadata. The gRPC service is entirely separate from the REST gateway and D3 did not touch it. Replace this header read with path-based extraction... except gRPC doesn't have URL paths in the same way. gRPC calls use service + method names, not paths. The database has to come from either:

(a) A metadata field (still a header-equivalent), OR
(b) A new field on every request proto

(a) is pragmatic. Rename the metadata header from `x-axon-database` to `x-axon-tenant-database` and carry a composite value `"{tenant}:{database}"`. This matches the REST composite slug. The gRPC service parses it and dispatches via the same `get_or_create_any` path.

Also add `x-axon-authorization` (or reuse the standard `authorization` metadata key) for JWT credentials. Run the JWT verify pipeline in the gRPC `check_auth` step before dispatching to the handler.

For this bead, the gRPC change is small:
- Rename the metadata constant
- Update `grpc_current_database` to parse the composite form
- Update the Rust + TS gRPC clients to send the composite form
- Add JWT auth path in `check_auth`

### 4. DatabaseRouter NOT swapped in (deferred — not needed right now)

D2 landed `DatabaseRouter` as a new primitive, but the gateway still uses `TenantRouter::get_or_create_any(slug)`. The smoke test confirmed this composite slug works correctly. Replacing TenantRouter with DatabaseRouter is a refactor that doesn't change behavior — defer it to a later cleanup bead. Not in this bead's scope.

## Scope summary

This bead does exactly three things:

1. Install B1's JWT middleware in the data-plane router, optional path (Authorization header present)
2. Populate F1's AuditAttribution at every audit write site when ResolvedIdentity is present
3. Migrate gRPC service.rs from x-axon-database header to the composite slug + optional JWT metadata

## Tests for this bead

### Integration test: crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs (NEW)

- create a JwtIssuer with a known key
- mint a JWT for a default user with grants on (default, default)
- start the in-process test server harness that gateway_test.rs uses
- send a request with Authorization: Bearer <jwt> to /tenants/default/databases/default/collections
- assert 200 (the JWT pipeline accepted + the handler saw ResolvedIdentity)

### Integration test: attribution populated

- send an entity-create request with a JWT
- query the audit log
- assert the audit entry has `attribution.user_id` set to the JWT sub

### gRPC test: composite slug

- send a CreateEntity gRPC request with metadata `x-axon-tenant-database: acme:orders`
- assert routing to the acme:orders database (check the storage file exists)

### Manual smoke test

Start the server, issue a JWT via `/control/tenants/default/credentials`, use the HttpAxonClient with the JWT, create an entity, verify the audit log has attribution. Write a short shell script under `scripts/cutover-smoke.sh` that does this end-to-end.

## Out of scope

- DatabaseRouter replacement — deferred
- OIDC federation — follow-up
- Signing key rotation — future ADR
- Removing legacy Identity/Role types from auth.rs — they still serve the --no-auth and Tailscale paths

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — install JWT middleware, thread AuditAttribution into audit writes (10-20 sites)
- crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs — construct JwtIssuer at startup
- crates/axon-server/src/service.rs — rename header constant, parse composite, add JWT path
- crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs — NEW
- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh — NEW
- Possibly sdk/typescript/src/client.ts — update gRPC metadata key if touching gRPC SDK surface

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 is canonical, just consume it
- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale path, out of scope for removal
- crates/axon-storage/* — no storage changes
- crates/axon-core/* — no type changes

## Verification

JwtClaims, Op, TenantId, TenantMember, TenantRole, User, UserId,\n+};\n+use axon_server::actor_scope::ActorScopeGuard;\n+use axon_server::auth::AuthContext;\n+use axon_server::auth_pipeline::JwtIssuer;\n+use axon_server::control_plane::ControlPlaneDb;\n+use axon_server::control_plane_routes::ControlPlaneState;\n+use axon_server::cors_config::CorsStore;\n+use axon_server::gateway::build_router_with_auth;\n+use axon_server::rate_limit::RateLimitConfig;\n+use axon_server::tenant_router::{TenantHandler, TenantRouter};\n+use axon_server::user_roles::UserRoleStore;\n+use axon_storage::adapter::StorageAdapter;\n+use axon_storage::memory::MemoryStorageAdapter;\n+\n+// ── Constants ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n+\n+const ISSUER: &str = \"cutover-issuer\";\n+const TENANT: &str = \"acme\";\n+const DATABASE: &str = \"orders\";\n+const COLLECTION: &str = \"items\";\n+const USER_ID: &str = \"u-cutover-01\";\n+const SECRET: &[u8] = b\"cutover-test-secret-32bytes-xxxxx\";\n+\n+fn now_secs() -> u64 {\n+    SystemTime::now()\n+        .duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH)\n+        .unwrap_or_default()\n+        .as_secs()\n+}\n+\n+// ── Test fixtures ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n+\n+/// Build the pre-seeded auth storage (user + tenant member).\n+fn make_auth_storage() -> MemoryStorageAdapter {\n+    let mut storage = MemoryStorageAdapter::default();\n+    storage.insert_user(User {\n+        id: UserId::new(USER_ID),\n+        display_name: \"Cutover User\".to_string(),\n+        email: None,\n+        created_at_ms: 0,\n+        suspended_at_ms: None,\n+    });\n+    storage.insert_tenant_member(TenantMember {\n+        tenant_id: TenantId::new(TENANT),\n+        user_id: UserId::new(USER_ID),\n+        role: TenantRole::Write,\n+    });\n+    storage\n+}\n+\n+/// Build a `ControlPlaneState` with jwt_issuer + auth storage so the optional\n+/// JWT verification layer is enabled in the HTTP router.\n+fn make_control_plane(issuer: Arc<JwtIssuer>, auth_storage: MemoryStorageAdapter) -> ControlPlaneState {\n+    let db = ControlPlaneDb::open_in_memory().expect(\"in-memory control-plane db should open\");\n+    let db = Arc::new(TokioMutex::new(db));\n+    let boxed: Box<dyn StorageAdapter + Send + Sync> = Box::new(auth_storage);\n+    let storage_arc: Arc<Mutex<Box<dyn StorageAdapter + Send + Sync>>> =\n+        Arc::new(Mutex::new(boxed));\n+    ControlPlaneState::new(db, PathBuf::from(\".\"), UserRoleStore::default(), CorsStore::default())\n+        .with_jwt_issuer(issuer)\n+        .with_storage(storage_arc)\n+}\n+\n+/// Build an `axum_test::TestServer` backed by an in-memory data-plane handler.\n+///\n+/// Returns the test server and the data-plane handler so tests can inspect the\n+/// audit log directly.\n+fn make_server(\n+    issuer: Arc<JwtIssuer>,\n+) -> (axum_test::TestServer, TenantHandler) {\n+    let auth_storage = make_auth_storage();\n+    let control_plane = make_control_plane(issuer, auth_storage);\n+\n+    let data_storage: Box<dyn StorageAdapter + Send + Sync> =\n+        Box::new(MemoryStorageAdapter::default());\n+    let handler: TenantHandler =\n+        Arc::new(TokioMutex::new(AxonHandler::new(data_storage)));\n+    let tenant_router = Arc::new(TenantRouter::single(handler.clone()));\n+\n+    let app = build_router_with_auth(\n+        tenant_router,\n+        \"memory\",\n+        None,\n+        AuthContext::no_auth(),\n+        RateLimitConfig::default(),\n+        ActorScopeGuard::default(),\n+        Some(control_plane),\n+        CorsStore::default(),\n+    );\n+\n+    (axum_test::TestServer::new(app), handler)\n+}\n+\n+fn issuer() -> Arc<JwtIssuer> {\n+    Arc::new(JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER.to_string()))\n+}\n+\n+fn write_grants() -> Grants {\n+    Grants {\n+        databases: vec![GrantedDatabase {\n+            name: DATABASE.to_string(),\n+            ops: vec![Op::Read, Op::Write],\n+        }],\n+    }\n+}\n+\n+fn read_only_grants() -> Grants {\n+    Grants {\n+        databases: vec![GrantedDatabase {\n+            name: DATABASE.to_string(),\n+            ops: vec![Op::Read],\n+        }],\n+    }\n+}\n+\n+fn make_token(issuer: &JwtIssuer, grants: Grants) -> String {\n+    let now = now_secs();\n+    let claims = JwtClaims {\n+        iss: ISSUER.to_string(),\n+        sub: USER_ID.to_string(),\n+        aud: TENANT.to_string(),\n+        jti: Uuid::now_v7().to_string(),\n+        iat: now,\n+        nbf: now,\n+        exp: now + 3600,\n+        grants,\n+    };\n+    issuer.issue(&claims).expect(\"token issuance should succeed\")\n+}\n+\n+fn entity_path(id: &str) -> String {\n+    format!(\"/tenants/{TENANT}/databases/{DATABASE}/entities/{COLLECTION}/{id}\")\n+}\n+\n+fn entity_body(value: i32) -> Value {\n+    json!({ \"data\": { \"value\": value } })\n+}\n+\n+// ── Test 1: No auth header falls through to no-auth mode ─────────────────────\n+\n+#[tokio::test]\n+async fn cutover_no_auth_header_falls_through_to_no_auth_201() {\n+    let jw = issuer();\n+    let (server, _handler) = make_server(jw);\n+\n+    // No Authorization header → goes through no-auth path → 201\n+    let resp = server\n+        .post(&entity_path(\"e-noauth-01\"))\n+        .json(&entity_body(1))\n+        .await;\n+    resp.assert_status(axum::http::StatusCode::CREATED);\n+}\n+\n+// ── Test 2: Valid JWT with write grant → 201 ─────────────────────────────────\n+\n+#[tokio::test]\n+async fn cutover_valid_jwt_write_grant_creates_entity_201() {\n+    let jw = issuer();\n+    let token = make_token(&jw, write_grants());\n+    let (server, _handler) = make_server(jw);\n+\n+    let resp = server\n+        .post(&entity_path(\"e-jwt-write-01\"))\n+        .add_header(\n+            axum::http::header::AUTHORIZATION,\n+            axum::http::HeaderValue::from_str(&format!(\"Bearer {token}\")).unwrap(),\n+        )\n+        .json(&entity_body(42))\n+        .await;\n+    resp.assert_status(axum::http::StatusCode::CREATED);\n+    let body: Value = resp.json();\n+    assert_eq!(body[\"entity\"][\"id\"].as_str().unwrap_or(\"\"), \"e-jwt-write-01\");\n+}\n+\n+// ── Test 3: JWT with read-only grant blocks POST → 403 ───────────────────────\n+\n+#[tokio::test]\n+async fn cutover_jwt_read_only_grant_blocks_post_403() {\n+    let jw = issuer();\n+    let token = make_token(&jw, read_only_grants());\n+    let (server, _handler) = make_server(jw);\n+\n+    let resp = server\n+        .post(&entity_path(\"e-jwt-read-01\"))\n+        .add_header(\n+            axum::http::header::AUTHORIZATION,\n+            axum::http::HeaderValue::from_str(&format!(\"Bearer {token}\")).unwrap(),\n+        )\n+        .json(&entity_body(1))\n+        .await;\n+    // JWT layer returns 403 because the write op is not in the read-only grants.\n+    resp.assert_status(axum::http::StatusCode::FORBIDDEN);\n+    let body: Value = resp.json();\n+    assert_eq!(\n+        body[\"error\"][\"code\"].as_str().unwrap_or(\"\"),\n+        \"op_not_granted\",\n+    );\n+}\n+\n+// ── Test 4: JWT write → attribution populated in audit entry ─────────────────\n+\n+#[tokio::test]\n+async fn cutover_audit_entry_has_attribution_after_jwt_write() {\n+    let jw = issuer();\n+    let token = make_token(&jw, write_grants());\n+    let (server, handler) = make_server(jw);\n+\n+    // Create the entity via JWT.\n+    let resp = server\n+        .post(&entity_path(\"e-attr-01\"))\n+        .add_header(\n+            axum::http::header::AUTHORIZATION,\n+            axum::http::HeaderValue::from_str(&format!(\"Bearer {token}\")).unwrap(),\n+        )\n+        .json(&entity_body(99))\n+        .await;\n+    resp.assert_status(axum::http::StatusCode::CREATED);\n+\n+    // Query the audit log directly from the data-plane handler.\n+    let audit_resp = handler\n+        .lock()\n+        .await\n+        .query_audit(QueryAuditRequest {\n+            database: None,\n+            collection: None,\n+            collection_ids: vec![],\n+            entity_id: None,\n+            actor: None,\n+            operation: Some(\"entity.create\".to_string()),\n+            since_ns: None,\n+            until_ns: None,\n+            after_id: None,\n+            limit: Some(10),\n+        })\n+        .expect(\"audit query should succeed\");\n+\n+    assert!(\n+        !audit_resp.entries.is_empty(),\n+        \"expected at least one audit entry after entity create\"\n+    );\n+\n+    let entry = audit_resp\n+        .entries\n+        .iter()\n+        .find(|e| e.entity_id.to_string() == \"e-attr-01\")\n+        .expect(\"entry for e-attr-01 must be present\");\n+\n+    let attribution = entry\n+        .attribution\n+        .as_ref()\n+        .expect(\"attribution must be set on JWT-authenticated write\");\n+\n+    assert_eq!(attribution.user_id, USER_ID, \"user_id must match JWT sub\");\n+    assert_eq!(attribution.tenant_id, TENANT, \"tenant_id must match JWT aud\");\n+    assert_eq!(attribution.auth_method, \"jwt\", \"auth_method must be 'jwt'\");\n+}\n+\n+// ── gRPC composite header parsing ────────────────────────────────────────────\n+\n+/// Verify that the composite `{tenant}:{database}` header value is parsed\n+/// correctly by `grpc_requested_database` We do this by starting a\n+    // minimal gRPC service and issuing a create_entity call with the header.\n+    use axon_server::service::{AxonServiceImpl, AxonServiceServer};\n+    use axon_server::service::proto;\n+    use proto::axon_service_client::AxonServiceClient;\n+    use tonic::metadata::MetadataValue;\n+\n+    let svc = AxonServiceImpl::new_in_memory();\n+    let listener });\n+\n+    tokio::time::sleep(std::time::Duration::from_millis(50)).await;\n+\n+    let mut client = AxonServiceClient::connect(format!(\"http://{addr}\"))\n+        .await\n+        .unwrap();\n+\n+    // Construct a request with the composite `tenant:database` header.\n+    let mut req = tonic::Request::new(proto::CreateEntityRequest {\n+        collection: \"x-axon-tenant-database\",\n+        MetadataValue::try_from(\"acme:orders\").unwrap(),\n+    );\n+\n+    let resp = client.create_entity(req).await.expect(\"grpc create should succeed\");\n+    let \"grpc-e-01\");\n+}","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"0a9f1446-c2e0-4db8-b50e-318c50111461","uuid":"9151c21d-5adf-4d7d-a724-fbf7f18dd1b3","timestamp":"2026-04-16T01:10:02.067Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"diff
